title: CCCL Runtime: A Modern C++ Runtime for CUDA | NVIDIA Technical Blog
gist: NVIDIA introduced CCCL runtime, a modern C++ alternative to the 20-year-old CUDA runtime API that uses strong typing, explicit dependencies and async-by-default semantics to make CUDA development safer and more composable, while remaining interoperable with existing CUDA code for incremental adoption.

insights.claim: NVIDIA is rebuilding the CUDA runtime API as CCCL runtime, a set of idiomatic modern-C++ headers (cuda/stream, cuda/buffer, cuda/launch) that incorporate 20 years of lessons rather than being bound by the original C source-compatibility constraints.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Lowering CUDA's developer friction widens NVIDIA's software moat; an easier programming model deepens lock-in as CUDA remains the chief switching cost protecting GPU market share.
insights.quote: We also took the opportunity to incorporate lessons learned over 20 years of CUDA evolution into the API design.

insights.claim: CCCL runtime targets the growing complexity of CUDA programs where multiple libraries share devices, streams and memory, providing APIs that compose cleanly and make dependencies explicit.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Signals NVIDIA's awareness that multi-library AI stacks (the modern GPU workload) strain the legacy API; tooling investment here protects the high end of the AI-compute market.
insights.quote: As CUDA programs grow more complex,  with multiple libraries sharing devices, streams, and memory, the need for APIs that compose cleanly and make dependencies explicit becomes more pressing.

themes.theme: Design principles: typing and explicit state

insights.claim: CCCL runtime replaces raw identifiers with dedicated strong types (a device is a device_ref, a stream is an object) to catch mistakes at compile time rather than runtime.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Fewer runtime errors lowers the cost of building production GPU software, reinforcing CUDA's developer-experience advantage over rival accelerator stacks.
insights.quote: Strong typing across the API helps catch mistakes at compile time rather than chasing them at runtime.

insights.claim: CCCL runtime deliberately removes implicit global state such as the current device and the default stream; all CCCL runtime streams are created non-blocking and the default-stream notion no longer applies.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Eliminating implicit state improves library composability, which matters as AI frameworks stack many CUDA libraries together; a quiet but real productivity lever for the ecosystem.
insights.quote: With no default stream in the API, the notion of a “blocking stream” no longer applies, so all CCCL runtime streams are created as non-blocking.

insights.claim: Following std::string / std::string_view, CCCL runtime gives most CUDA objects an owning type and a non-owning _ref type, with implicit conversion from raw handles for incremental adoption alongside existing code.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Incremental interoperability removes the rewrite barrier, maximizing the install base that can migrate without breaking existing CUDA codebases.
insights.locator: Resource ownership: Owning types and refs

insights.claim: CCCL runtime makes APIs asynchronous by default via a simple convention: any API taking a stream as its first argument operates in stream order, and NVIDIA does not plan to provide synchronous counterparts.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Steering all developers toward async stream-ordered execution pushes the ecosystem toward the patterns that maximize GPU utilization, indirectly lifting realized performance on NVIDIA hardware.
insights.quote: If an API takes a stream as its first argument, it operates in stream order.

insights.claim: CCCL runtime makes memory pools and stream-ordered allocation the default, with plans to remove non-stream-ordered fallback entirely once pool support is universal across CUDA versions and platforms.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Signals NVIDIA's roadmap to make memory pooling mandatory; performance-tuned defaults reduce the gap between naive and expert CUDA code.
insights.quote: Memory pooling and less frequent synchronization points are in most cases essential to reach maximum performance

insights.claim: Buffer initialization is mandatory unless explicitly opted out with cuda::no_init, treating uninitialized device memory as a known source of hard-to-diagnose bugs requiring an explicit choice.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Safer defaults reduce debugging cost for GPU developers, part of the cumulative DX advantage that keeps CUDA dominant.
insights.quote: Uninitialized device memory is a common source of hard-to-diagnose bugs, so we chose to require an explicit opt-out rather than making it the silent default.

insights.claim: cuda::launch moves compile-time information (e.g. block size as a template argument to cuda::distribute) from the host launch site into device code via the type system, letting kernels skip runtime block-size queries.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Compile-time specialization can yield faster kernels with less boilerplate; another lever raising baseline efficiency of CUDA code on NVIDIA GPUs.
insights.quote: The most novel aspect of cuda::launch is how it moves compile-time information from the host launch site into device code through the type system.

insights.claim: CCCL runtime introduces kernel functors (structs with __device__ operator()) that auto-deduce template arguments, whereas legacy __global__ functions require explicit instantiation.
insights.speaker: Piotr Ciolkosz
insights.so_what: Reduces template boilerplate, a marginal but real DX improvement reinforcing CUDA's ergonomics lead.
